title: Why the World Cup Keeps Eluding China
gist: FP deputy editor James Palmer argues that Xi Jinping's 15-year, billions-dollar campaign to turn China into a soccer power has failed because the same pattern afflicting other 'national priority' sectors — heavy money inflows with too few institutions capable of policing them — has made Chinese soccer's domestic league rich but corrupt, keeping talent from developing abroad and driving repeated purges that never fix the underlying system.

insights.claim: China has qualified for the World Cup only once, in 2002, when it was eliminated in the group stage without scoring a goal; despite the 2026 tournament expanding from 32 to 48 teams with a record nine Asian Football Confederation slots, China still failed to qualify, losing out to newcomers such as Jordan and Uzbekistan.
insights.speaker: James Palmer
insights.so_what: Quantifies the persistent gap between China's soccer investment and results, a useful baseline for judging whether the 2050 'soccer superpower' target is realistic.
insights.locator: China has reached the World Cup only once

insights.claim: Xi Jinping, a longtime soccer fan, stated in 2011, before becoming president, three goals for Chinese soccer — qualify for, host, and win the World Cup — and 15 years and billions of dollars in investment later, China is no closer to any of them.
insights.speaker: James Palmer
insights.so_what: A direct, falsifiable benchmark of a named Xi personal-priority policy failing despite sustained state spending — relevant to gauging execution risk in other Xi-designated 'priority' sectors.
insights.locator: Soccer is a personal priority of Chinese President Xi Jinping

insights.claim: China has a state-backed push to turn itself into a 'first-class soccer superpower' by 2050, running alongside repeated anti-corruption crackdowns, none of which have solved the sport's structural problems.
insights.speaker: James Palmer
insights.so_what: Establishes a long-dated, publicly stated state target that can be tracked as a proxy for the effectiveness of China's top-down industrial-policy model more broadly.
insights.locator: a massive, state-backed push to turn China

themes.theme: Why the money doesn't translate to results

insights.claim: The Chinese Super League is lucrative and one of the world's best-attended domestic leagues, which paradoxically keeps talented players at home rather than seeking experience abroad, while incompetence and corruption inside the league cause that talent to go to waste.
insights.speaker: James Palmer
insights.so_what: A domestic league rich enough to retain talent but too dysfunctional to develop it is a specific, identifiable bottleneck — distinct from a simple lack-of-money story that would otherwise attract capital.
insights.locator: Part of the problem is that the Chinese domestic league

insights.claim: Not a single player on China's current men's national team plays for a foreign club, compared with eight members of the US squad who play in Major League Soccer, a relatively weak league; Chinese players often go abroad in youth careers but rarely stay overseas as seniors.
insights.speaker: James Palmer
insights.so_what: A concrete, comparative talent-pipeline metric showing China's domestic system actively pulls players back rather than exporting them into more competitive environments.
insights.locator: Not a single player on the current Chinese national team

insights.claim: China cannot replicate Qatar's strategy of naturalizing foreign players to quickly build a competitive squad because Chinese soccer's persistent racism and ethnic nationalism create a hostile environment for players who are not ethnically Chinese.
insights.speaker: James Palmer
insights.so_what: Rules out a fast, capital-driven fix that has worked elsewhere, implying China's soccer problem is a longer-horizon institutional and cultural one rather than a financing gap.
insights.locator: China also can’t easily replicate the model used by Qatar

insights.claim: The Chinese women's national team, the Steel Roses, has appeared in every FIFA Women's World Cup since 1991 except 2011, a sharp contrast with the men's team's near-total failure, implying China's soccer problems are specific to the men's professional pipeline rather than soccer culture broadly.
insights.speaker: James Palmer
insights.so_what: Isolates the failure to the men's professional system (money, corruption, incentives) rather than to Chinese sporting culture or talent base in general.

insights.claim: In January 2026, Chinese authorities handed lifetime bans to 73 players and officials for match-fixing and bribery, including former national coach Li Tie, who was already serving a prison sentence — the latest round in a cycle where officials who rose to power after earlier corruption purges are themselves later purged.
insights.speaker: James Palmer
insights.so_what: Evidence that anti-corruption enforcement in Chinese soccer is cyclical rather than structural, so investors or sponsors should not treat any single crackdown as a durable fix.
insights.locator: In January, Chinese authorities announced sweeping punishments

insights.claim: Chinese soccer's corruption is an instance of a broader pattern that has also hit the People's Liberation Army Rocket Force and the state-backed semiconductor 'Big Fund': once Beijing designates a sector a national priority, the resulting inflow of money creates strong incentives for corruption that few institutions are capable enough to expose.
insights.speaker: James Palmer
insights.so_what: A generalizable governance risk framework: any Chinese sector suddenly designated a 'national priority' (soccer, semiconductors, military modernization) should be discounted for elevated corruption and execution risk regardless of stated capital commitments.
insights.locator: A familiar pattern has played out across multiple sectors

insights.claim: Foreign coaches brought in to revive China's national team are repeatedly constrained by entrenched interests and a sports system where Chinese Communist Party officials have final say, unlike South Korea's 2002 approach of giving Dutch coach Guus Hiddink total authority over selection and strategy, which led to a semifinal run.
insights.speaker: James Palmer
insights.so_what: Identifies a governance fix (delegated authority to technical experts) that worked for a comparable Asian program but that China's political system structurally resists, marking a durable competitive disadvantage.
insights.locator: South Korea, by contrast, gave Dutch manager

themes.theme: Where hope remains, and its limits

insights.claim: China's 2008 Olympic success relied on a highly centralized approach directing young athletes into individual sports like shooting or diving, which require only a handful of elite competitors and far less money or interaction with existing institutions — a model that does not transfer to soccer, which needs deep, broad talent pools.
insights.speaker: James Palmer
insights.so_what: Explains why China's proven Olympic playbook (centralized, low-headcount elite development) cannot simply be repointed at soccer, a structural constraint on any future state-led fix.
insights.locator: When China made a big Olympic push

insights.claim: Grassroots village soccer leagues have surged in popularity in China over roughly the past two years, raising hopes of finding untapped talent among 1.4 billion people, but Palmer predicts any grassroots success will likely be absorbed by existing sporting authorities and dragged into the same institutional problems that have long plagued the sport.
insights.speaker: James Palmer
insights.so_what: Tempers optimism around any bottom-up Chinese soccer revival narrative — the author's base case is that existing dysfunctional institutions will co-opt and dilute grassroots gains.
insights.locator: the depressing likelihood is that any grassroots success
